speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. They need some space between takes. That's why. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We stopped when you was explaining us about the German woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we continue with the German woman, I just want to ask you, what was the feeling? And who liberated that camp, actually? How'd this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. Definitely, the Russians. We were a few days in no man's land. You know what I mean? Because the Germans left, and the Russians didn't arrive yet. And all of a sudden-- and we're trying to cope somehow the best we could. And our leaders of, you know, Jewish people who were kind of leaders, they opened store rooms, and they give us some extra bread, and so on, so we were not hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, we see-- also, the word of mouth, whoever was not there, herded everybody [INAUDIBLE]-- a little small Russian soldier comes on a little horse, and his feet are like with-- with-- with rags? You know what I mean? I don't remember even if he had shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, everybody surrounded him. The women were kissing his feet and his hands, and everything. And this was the first. And then, of course, you know, the other Russians came with the authorities. And we had to register whoever wanted to go back. You know, we had to register, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back to the woman. And then what's happened with this good Nazi woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: The good Nazi woman-- we lived there, and it was a beautiful villa, and everything. And one day, when the conditions-- she became accustomed, already, to the Russians being there. She was not afraid anymore. She asked us to leave, the good Nazi woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you believe that there were good Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. If you could count on fingers of two hands, that's it. I knew-- I knew-- because you see, it's something else to help one person, maybe we-- a Polack knew a German in [INAUDIBLE] who helped me a couple of times. But he helped me only. That doesn't mean that he was a good Nazi. But a person who-- like Oscar Schindler, for instance-- who saved 1,300 Jews -- by manipulating -- the other Germans, and-- AND feeding us, and saving us, this is an accomplishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you went back-- after this, you went back to Cracow? What's after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. We never went back to Cracow, no. We stayed for about-- with another group of ours. Some people left right away. We stayed for another six weeks, because Poldeck ate this terrific first soup after the war, and he got terribly sick. He was as sick as I was in Auschwitz. And we had no sanitary conditions of any kind-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed for another six weeks. And already then we-- well, we had contact with the town. you know. We could go freely wherever we wanted. And they were saying there was a terrible pogrom in Kielce in Poland. You know, right after the war, when Jewish people started to come back, they killed maybe, I don't know, 40, I think, in the town of Kielce.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, we thought we would like to go, if possible, to Palestine. So we wanted to kind of try what we can do. And Poldeck left me with this group of people. And he went to Budapest. He left me in Bratislava. We went to Bratislava by train. He left me there, and he went to Budapest. In Budapest, he met a friend. And he said, bring Mila right away, and, you know, that friend was saved on Aryan papers. He survived the war in Budapest. And we thought that arriving Budapest, we can get to Bucharest, and then we can go somehow-- this was illegal, because Israel didn't exist yet. This was Palestine, and the British did everything possible to-- not to let the Jews in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Budapest, and then we realized it will be very difficult. The British established a camp on Cyprus for the illegal immigrants. And we had enough of camps. So we turned back, and we went to Czechoslovakia again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Czechoslovakia, we crossed the border. We wanted to be in the American zone. So we crossed the border, then we found ourselves in Munich, where Mr. Schindler was, and a whole group of our friends from camps, and from the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a camp? A DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't live in-- there were a few DP camps, but we never lived. We rented a room from some German lady, and we lived this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I told you, we got those pieces of fabric. Those were-- you know, you could get-- you know, if you sold it, you got quite an amount of money. So this was our initial thing. But when we were living in Budapest for three months, we thought we will be able to-- to-- to get to-- to Bucharest, and then to Palestine. When we lived there, again, the only way of living was doing the black market. So you-- the-- the Russians wanted a watch, and they had flour. You know, this is how it went. So we-- this is how we lived. And I was able to buy myself a few things to wear, which everybody admired in Munich, because they were-- they were made in Budapest, and they were much nicer. Everybody copied my one dress and my one suit. And then we came, and we applied to the American authorities for-- to-- to immigrate to the United States. This was-- we came the end of 1945 to Munich. And in April of 1947, we were lucky enough to get the papers and get on the ship and come to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were one year in Munich [INAUDIBLE], one year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were one and a half-- one and a half years, yes. Poldeck was working for [INAUDIBLE] for the United Relief, Yeah. And he was doing a wonderful job there. He was running schools and camps for DP children. Unfortunately, there were no Jewish DP children-- maybe a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to New York, and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to New York, and life was very, very difficult for us. Again--

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke English already.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took English lessons before the war. When I came from Vienna, you know, my mother insisted that I take private lessons, because in schools, in Poland, you had a choice of French, or German. And I used to be very fluent in French. That's why I couldn't understand why my mother sent me to Vienna, you know? But in those days, you didn't tell your mother what to do. You know, you just obeyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But English I learned after, you know, just before the war started. So I-- I could-- I was very shy. So I was very-- I didn't want to speak. But my husband didn't speak English, didn't learn English, but he knew the whole street in one day. You know? And people were wonderful to us-- the butcher, the baker, everybody, the neighbors, everybody. And people in New York are really very nice and warm-- very nice. And how it happened that you came to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived three and a half years in New York. I was working there in a department store, and picking up the dresses from the floor. And this is how I learned English, because I couldn't speak anything else. And we did not have a place to live, because we rented part of a house from a gentleman who lost his wife. And then he remarried again, so we had no place to live. And you wouldn't believe it. No one wanted to rent us a place with a baby. Fred was born in 1950 in New York-- no one. And we had friends--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your son, first son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our son. And we had friends already who moved from New York to California. They had enough of New York, and they kept writing to us and calling us. They said in the morning-- California in the morning, you can go swimming, or skiing. And in the afternoon, you can go swimming and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took a nine-month-old baby, got on the train. And again, a neighbor of ours in New York-- next door neighbor-- had such confidence in us that he loaned us money. And Poldeck still today says, you should have taken more. He wanted to give us more, but we didn't take much. And this is how-- how we started here. You opened a-- [INAUDIBLE] get back to the leather shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was one mistake we made-- the leather. shop. Well, Poldeck couldn't teach, because he-- we needed to make a living right away. So he already had a little business with this friend of his in New York. They were repairing handbags. And I was the delivery girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was going-- they had accounts to stores, you know, like good stores in that area, you know? So I used to deliver the bags, either on the bus, or walking. And one time, I was having a whole bunch of handbags, and the paper bag in which they put it broke, you know? So I had to pick them up from the ground, and-- and I went to a cleaning shop over there, and I asked him to please sell me a paper bag. And he took $0.05 from me. Thank God I had the $0.05 on me at that time. He took $0.05 for the paper bag. When you came here, so he immediately opened a-- a bag--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. He actually-- he opened a repair shop, just like the one we had in New York on Little Santa Monica near Linden. And this is when he had his nicest customers, because the whole elite-- you know, all the-- the celebrities, so-called, lived, at that time, in Beverly Hills. Now, they move.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our daughter was born in Los Angeles in 1956, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So slowly you bought an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Rent ?] apartment? Not right away. Not right away, no. We lived-- first of all, we lived near Alvarado and Third Street. We had an apartment there. And it was very nice. Nothing wrong with that at the time. And then we moved on Burton Way. And then-- and then, what did we do then? Oh! Then we bought our first little house-- a little Spanish house, for $12,500, $2,000 down. And then we traded-- I mean, we sold this house. And the equity that we earned during that time, we bought the next house. And then we bought an apartment building here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today-- today, you have already grandchildren?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have two grandchildren. Samantha is 12, and Matthew is 9. They're all from your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from my son. My daughter didn't get married yet, and I'm very angry about it. But I can't-- [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] I can't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us, not to us, but to the people that will watch this tape one day, do you have some message to say about your life during Holocaust, during Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, to the people who-- the message to non-Jewish people is first of all, never, never to be prejudiced against any group of people, any group of people, because that's the worst thing, which can start such a tragedy as our tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, on the other hand, believe in the goodness of human nature, because along the way, through our journey, so to say, during the war, we met quite a number of very nice people, and even in the camp, you know? I remember my friends. We were so helpful. I think that we are more selfish now having so much than we were at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were helpful to one another. We were understanding of one another. One has to believe in the goodness of human nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your message?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think why you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survived? I guess--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always say, and my husband says the same thing, it's pure, sheer luck. You couldn't be smart during the war. The whole thing was a matter of luck, to be at the right place and the right time, or not to be in the wrong place at the wrong time. That's all. You couldn't be smart. I knew smart people who perished. And-- and some people who went, like me most of the time, you know-- wherever I was pushed, I went, and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, you have a lot of friends from it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we have lots of friends. Yeah, we always had friends. When we came to Munich, we had friends. When we came to New York, we had-- we always had lots of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. And now we will let--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're more than  welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us some pictures? You have some photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. This is the oldest picture that I have of my father and his family. He is sitting to the left. His name is-- was Dr. Zygfryd Lewinson, his mother, Iohanna, his sister, Naomi, and his brother, Moris. And I used to lie to my friends that this is my real brother, because I was terribly upset that I was an only child. So I used to make up stories. Yeah, they used to live in Paris, and they all died in the-- I would say in the early '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, which I got very recently, maybe a year ago. And I didn't have it till then. And my mother was conducting at the time. This is a picture done in 1932. I was 12 years old, and I remembered that she was conducting a seminar on hygiene to a group of-- of-- of hairdressers from our city. And my father is the first one on the left. She's the fourth one from the left also. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is in the city of Lodz, in my city. My father is sitting, the first one on the left, and my mother is in the middle, the fourth one from the left. And they were conducting the seminar on hygiene to a group-- a group of hairdressers from my city. This is our wedding picture. We got married in Cracow, Poland, on July 7, 1940. And this was-- we were married in a very small synagogue in the Jewish section of Cracow by the only rabbi who existed, I think, who performed the ceremonies at the time. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi's name? I don't know. [LAUGHS] Oh, [INAUDIBLE] my husband, Leopold, known as Poldeck. And my name is Ludmilla.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a picture made in 1946, or beginning of 1947, in Munich, Germany, when-- shortly after the war. And this is a group surrounding Mr. Oskar Schindler, who saved most of these people on this photograph lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one to the left is [NON-ENGLISH] Rosner, who was saved by Mr. Schindler. She lives in New York now. And next to her is Mr. Edmund Horowitz, who I think emigrated to Argentina about the same time as Mr. Schindler. Unfortunately, he's not alive. The one in the middle in the white blouse, that's myself, Ludmilla Page. Next to me is Oskar Schindler. Next to him is a friend of ours who lives in Germany, I think, still or now in Israel. Her name is Rose Springer. Her name from-- maiden name, by which she was known in camp, was [NON-ENGLISH]. The little girl sitting, oh-- and the lady at the bottom is my dear friend-- was my dear friend-- Pauline Bloch. The little boy, whose head only we can see, is Al Rosner, a son of [NON-ENGLISH] and Henry Rosner, who was saved-- was in Mr Schindler's camp also for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to them is that the of Edmund Horowitz, [NON-ENGLISH] Horowitz, who lives now in Argentina. And she was saved as a little child, a little younger than here, by Mr. Schindler. OK? At this time it was Pfefferberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Munich by my husband Leopold Page, at the time known as Pfefferberg. And this is a great souvenir for all of us. This picture was taken in New York in Long Island-- Astoria, Long Island. This was in front of our first real apartment, furnished apartment. And it must be the year 19-- maybe '49. This was before our son was born. Must be '48 or '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ludmilla Page is my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's her natural habitat, you know? This is our daughter, Marie Page. She-- with her horse, Wendy, which she wants to consider my granddaughter. [LAUGHS] She lives here in Los Angeles, thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done-- I think last year, because the horse-- she has the horse two years. So I think it must-- must have been about last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1993 Or '92. This is a picture of our children-- our son, Fred Page and his wife, Judy Page. This was done probably after they got married. And they're married 17 years, so it must be about 15 years ago or so. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in 19--?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should remember. I don't. Now it's '94, less 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's about 1974.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. In late '70s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a picture of our-- my-- our granddaughter, Samantha Page It was done, I believe, two years ago. Right now she's 12 years old, and she's an absolute joy of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) What year? What year? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1992 the picture was made. This is, again, a picture of our granddaughter, Samantha Page, from 1992, with some strange-looking creatures. And she's now 12 years old. Excuse me. Oh, this is our grandson, Matthew Page, who is a baseball player, and a soccer player, and the most he loves basketball. He's 9 years old now.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) Where are when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture was made last year, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.
